narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 65.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile shows some strong points, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical student's 3.21—and an LSAT score of 37, just a bit higher than the average of 36.70. You're from an upper-middle income family, identify as male and White (both common in the applicant pool), and plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students. However, your school decile rankings stand out as unusually low: you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both Year 1 and Year 3, compared to averages around the middle of the pack at 5.71 and 5.53. These academic rankings were the most influential factors in the model's decision, each pushing the prediction toward expecting bar failure rather than success. Your LSAT score came next in influence, also nudging the outlook toward failure despite being slightly above average. Family income followed closely, contributing in the same direction for your situation, then your racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status—all playing smaller but still negative roles relative to typical applicants. In essence, while your test scores and background align decently with peers who pass, your consistently low class rankings signaled higher risk to the model, outweighing those positives. Compared to students who typically get approved, your deciles are in the lowest percentile, which dramatically shifts the odds against you. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic performance—perhaps through targeted study habits or extra courses to climb those school deciles, as that's the most changeable factor driving this outcome. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also help offset the prediction, since it ranked highly in influence. With dedication to these areas, you could realign your profile toward the stronger applicants who pass the bar. Keep pushing forward; many overcome early hurdles like this.
